chunk_summary: Explores actionable pediatric phenotypes in nutrigenomics, emphasizing genetic testing for conditions like familial hypercholesterolemia and syndromic obesity, with a focus on structured care and actionability in childhood management.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of nutrigenomics and nutrigenetics in pediatric care.
Identify high-yield phenotypes for genetic testing in nutrition-related conditions.
Apply guidelines like NICE and IAP for structured genetic testing and cascade screening.
Evaluate the actionability of genetic testing results in pediatric nutrition management.
key_facts: Familial hypercholesterolemia genetic testing supports cascade screening and early risk stratification.
IAP guidelines recommend genetic testing for early-onset obesity (<5 years) with hyperphagia and syndromic features.
Genetic testing should focus on red-flag phenotypes, not routine weight management.
Nutrigenomics studies diet–gene expression interactions, while nutrigenetics examines genetic variants modifying dietary responses.
AAP and ACMG policies emphasize testing only when management changes are expected in childhood.
reference_quote: Genetic testing is for red flags, not routine weight management. (IAP childhood obesity guideline, 2023)
document_key_topics: Nutrigenomics and nutrigenetics in pediatric care
Genetic testing for syndromic and monogenic obesity
Actionability in pediatric genetic test stewardship
